What is ESG? Explore Environmental, Social, Governance

===============

What is Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)?
===================================================

On this page
------------

*   [What is ESG?](https://www.esgbc.ca/what-is-esg/#what "How buildings cause pollution")
*   [Why is ESG important?](https://www.esgbc.ca/what-is-esg/#why)
*   [ESG pillars](https://www.esgbc.ca/what-is-esg/#pillars)
*   [ESG 101 guide](https://www.esgbc.ca/what-is-esg/#guide)

![Image 2: Prince Rupert, view from above of boat on ocean and forest/mountains to the right](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2023/11/RS2005_Prince-Rupert-photograph-bc-overview175-pr2-1024x683.jpg)

![Image 3: ESG conceptualized: hands holding holding a seedling with an overlay of icons symbolizing Environmental, Social, and Governance.](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2024/01/iStock-1360783154-1024x641.jpg)

**What is ESG?**
----------------

Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) is a comprehensive framework that looks at organizational impacts on the environment; the treatment of employees, stakeholders and community members; and addresses the quality of governance structures.

These are important factors for businesses in B.C. and go beyond financial accomplishment by creating a deeper set of considerations. By setting and sharing ESG goals, businesses can attract investors, build customer loyalty, and ensure long-term resilience and success, all while contributing to a more transparent and sustainable future.

**Why is ESG important?**
-------------------------

Embracing ESG can help businesses mitigate risk, thereby creating efficiencies and long-term value. Setting ESG goals is important for organizations, as it demonstrates they are ready to meet the future demands of a changing business environment.

Companies that integrate ESG principles:

*   Open doors to new markets
*   Attract funding and investment
*   Are better prepared to meet regulatory and reporting requirements
*   Build trust with partners, investors, consumers, and community
*   Strengthen recruitment
*   Foster innovation

![Image 4: Person fueling a vehicle at a hydrogen fueling station designed and fabricated by Powertech Labs of Surrey.](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2023/12/powertech-labs-photograph-cleantech.jpg)

**ESG is made up of three key pillars:**
----------------------------------------

Each pillar of the ESG framework includes measurable topics. Setting goals across all three pillars contributes to long-term, sustainable success.

![Image 5: Squamish, statue of two fish mounted on a rock next to a river with mountains in background.](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2023/12/rs2215-squamish-river.jpg)

![Image 6: Icon of two leaves.](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2024/01/icon_enviro.png)
### Environmental

The **Environmental** pillar assesses a company’s impact on the planet and its natural resources. Key topics under this pillar include:

*   Carbon footprint
*   Energy efficiency
*   Water usage
*   Waste management
*   Biodiversity

![Image 7: Icon of two adults and two children holding hands.](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2024/01/icon_social.png)
### ****Social****

The **Social** pillar examines how a business affects individuals and communities and how it manages relationships with various stakeholders. Key topics include:

*   Diversity and inclusion
*   Labour practices and human rights
*   Employee and community engagement
*   Health and safety
*   Supply chain practices

![Image 8: happy people in a community garden.](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2024/01/social-image.jpg)

![Image 9](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2024/01/iStock-1170078981-1024x683.jpg)

![Image 10: Icon of a handshake.](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2024/01/icon_govern.png)
### ******Governance******

The **Governance** pillar evaluates the frameworks and processes a business employs to uphold ethical standards, ensure transparency, and maintain accountability. Key topics include:

*   Shareholder rights
*   Executive compensation
*   Board diversity
*   Anti-corruption policies
*   Ethical business practices

**ESG 101 Guide**
-----------------

[![Image 11: cover of the ESG 101 guide.](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2024/01/101-guide-cover.jpg)](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2024/02/ESG-101-Guide_Feb2024.pdf)

Our comprehensive guide is an essential resource and great first step in navigating the world of ESG. Perfect for small and medium-sized businesses in B.C., this guide will help you understand what it means and why it matters, learn how to implement ESG strategies and discover how to measure and report on progress.

What’s inside:

*   Definitions and related terms
*   Step-by-step guidance on starting your ESG journey
*   Tips for how to identify which topics matter to you
*   Insights on international ESG reporting frameworks and standards
*   Real-world examples relevant to B.C. businesses

[Download the guide (PDF, 0.7MB)](https://www.esgbc.ca/app/uploads/sites/907/2024/02/ESG-101-Guide_Feb2024.pdf)

--- SOURCE 3: Environmental Governance Structures → Term ---
URL: https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/term/environmental-governance-structures/

SUMMARY:
The academic definition of Environmental Governance Structures can be articulated as the ensemble of institutions, instruments, and processes that shape interactions among actors and between actors and nature in relation to environmental and natural resource management issues.

FULL CONTENT:
![Pollution → Sustainability Directory](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Pollution-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Pollution → Sustainability Directory](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Pollution-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Environmental Governance Structures](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/environmental-governance-structures-term-image.webp)

# Environmental Governance Structures

![The image represents environmental degradation through a divided organic form showcasing pollution impact and habitat destruction. Visualized through contrasting colors and textures, it highlights the urgent need for sustainable practices climate action and effective carbon footprint reduction to mitigate ecological damage. The complex structure shows areas of potential environmental remediation.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/visualizing-environmental-degradation-a-complex-organic-form-depicting.jpg "Visualizing Environmental Degradation a Complex Organic Form Depicting Pollution Impact and Sustainable Inaction")

## Fundamentals

To understand **Environmental [Governance Meaning → Governance, in an ESG context, refers to the systems of rules, practices, and processes by which a company is directed and controlled.](https://esg.sustainability-directory.com/)  Structures**, it’s essential to begin with a foundational understanding of what they represent. At its simplest, [Environmental Governance Meaning → Environmental Governance: Systems directing human-environment interaction, balancing rules, actors, and sustainability.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/term/environmental-governance/)  Structures can be defined as the **framework of rules, practices, and institutions** that shape how humans interact with and manage the environment. This encompasses a wide spectrum of actions, from international treaties designed to combat climate change to local zoning laws determining land use. The initial **statement** we can make is that these structures are not static; they are constantly evolving in response to new environmental challenges, scientific discoveries, and societal values.

A crucial aspect in this **definition** is recognizing that governance extends beyond just government actions. It includes the roles of businesses, non-governmental organizations (NGOs), communities, and individuals in influencing environmental outcomes.

Environmental Governance Structures are the basic rules and organizations that guide our interactions with the environment.

Let’s further **clarify** this by considering the various components. Imagine a company deciding to reduce its carbon emissions. This action is influenced by several Environmental Governance Structures. First, there might be government regulations, such as carbon taxes or emissions standards, compelling the company to act.

Second, market-based mechanisms, like carbon trading schemes, could provide economic incentives. Third, pressure from stakeholders → consumers, investors, and environmental groups → can drive corporate environmental responsibility. Each of these factors represents a different facet of the overall governance structure. The **designation** of certain areas as protected zones, for example national parks, is another concrete **illustration** of Environmental [Governance Structures Meaning → Governance Structures are frameworks shaping actions towards sustainability, encompassing policies, processes, and institutions.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/term/governance-structures/)  in action. These designations involve legal frameworks, management plans, and enforcement mechanisms, all designed to conserve biodiversity and natural resources.

![This image presents a twig meticulously bound with dried plant fibers, reflecting a commitment to resource conservation and sustainable design principles. The rough texture and earth tones evoke a sense of rustic charm, while the composition explores environmental impact through material usage. It serves as a reminder of our responsibility to embrace eco conscious practices, mitigate pollution, and reduce our carbon footprint through waste reduction strategies and sustainable material selection.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-craft-a-twig-and-fiber-bundle-exploring-environmental-impact-and.jpg "Sustainable Craft a Twig and Fiber Bundle Exploring Environmental Impact and Resource Depletion Aesthetics")

### Key Elements of Basic Environmental Governance

To deepen our **explanation** at this fundamental level, we can identify several core elements that commonly appear across different Environmental Governance Structures:

Understanding these fundamental elements provides a solid basis for appreciating the complexities of Environmental Governance Structures as we progress to more advanced levels. The **meaning** of Environmental Governance Structures at this stage is about establishing the basic building blocks of environmental management and recognizing the diverse actors involved beyond just governmental bodies. The **intention** behind these structures is to guide human behavior in a way that minimizes environmental harm and promotes sustainability.

![A collection of discarded containers illustrates environmental degradation and pollution resulting from unsustainable consumption. Earthy tones and decaying materials underscore the impact of waste on the environment. The chaotic arrangement emphasizes the urgent need for better waste management practices, recycling initiatives, and sustainable solutions to minimize environmental damage, promote environmental stewardship and foster a circular economy contributing to a more sustainable future.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/environmental-degradation-a-close-up-view-of-discarded-containers-highlighting.jpg "Environmental Degradation a Close up View of Discarded Containers Highlighting Pollution and Unsustainable Practices")

## Intermediate

Moving to an intermediate understanding of **Environmental Governance Structures** requires a deeper **interpretation** of their functions and complexities. At this level, we recognize that these structures are not merely about setting rules, but also about navigating intricate interactions between different sectors, scales, and actors. We can **explain** Environmental Governance Structures as the dynamic and evolving systems through which societies make and implement decisions concerning the environment.

This **description** moves beyond the simple definition to acknowledge the inherent dynamism and complexity of these systems. The **implication** here is that effective environmental governance is not a static achievement but a continuous process of [adaptation Meaning → Adaptation involves adjusting to the current and future effects of climate change, building resilience in our systems and communities.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/)  and improvement.

Intermediate Environmental Governance Structures are dynamic systems navigating complex interactions to manage environmental decisions.

Consider the challenge of managing a shared resource like a river basin that crosses multiple political boundaries. Environmental Governance Structures in this [scenario Meaning → A scenario is a potential future outcome based on a coherent set of assumptions, a key tool for analysis on the Prism domain.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  must address the competing interests of different regions or countries, various water users (agriculture, industry, domestic), and diverse environmental concerns (pollution, biodiversity loss, water scarcity). This situation **clarifies** that governance often involves negotiating trade-offs and finding common ground among stakeholders with differing priorities. The **specification** of water rights, the establishment of joint management committees, and the implementation of transboundary pollution control measures are all components of Environmental Governance Structures designed to address such challenges.

![Close up showing interconnected root-like structures coated in particulate matter, representing a fragile ecosystem under environmental stress. The presence of microplastics as tiny beads emphasizes pervasive pollution. A golden, coiled object symbolizes human impact, urging reflection on sustainable solutions for ecological restoration.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/environmental-impact-of-microplastic-pollution-a-close-up-showing-degradation.jpg "Environmental Impact of Microplastic Pollution a Close up Showing Degradation and Sustainable Concerns")

### Exploring Complexity and Interconnectedness

To further **explicate** the intermediate level of Environmental Governance Structures, we need to examine several key dimensions that highlight their complexity:

At this intermediate stage, the **meaning** of Environmental Governance Structures expands to encompass the management of complexity and interconnectedness. The **significance** lies in understanding how these structures can be designed and implemented to effectively address environmental challenges that span multiple scales, sectors, and jurisdictions. The **intention** becomes not just to set rules, but to create systems that are flexible, integrated, and capable of adapting to changing environmental conditions and societal needs. The **connotation** now includes a focus on process and system design, rather than just static rules.

Effective intermediate Environmental Governance Structures manage complexity through multi-level, cross-sectoral, and adaptive approaches, often utilizing market mechanisms.

![The image presents a close-up of a soiled pipe fitting, symbolizing environmental pollution and highlighting the impact of unsustainable industrial activities. Particulate matter coats the metal surface, a visual representation of environmental degradation and the urgency for sustainable solutions. The photograph emphasizes the ecological footprint of industrial processes, advocating for cleaner production and waste reduction strategies.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/contaminated-pipe-end-illustrating-environmental-pollution-impact-and.jpg "Contaminated Pipe End Illustrating Environmental Pollution Impact and Unsustainable Waste Disposal Practices Raising Sustainability Concerns")

## Academic

From an academic standpoint, **Environmental Governance Structures** necessitate a rigorous and critical examination, moving beyond descriptive accounts to engage with theoretical underpinnings, normative dimensions, and empirical assessments of effectiveness. The academic **definition** of Environmental Governance Structures can be articulated as the ensemble of institutions, instruments, and processes that shape interactions among actors and between actors and nature in relation to environmental and natural resource management issues. This **statement** emphasizes the relational and process-oriented nature of governance.

This **interpretation** further extends to encompass [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  dynamics, equity concerns, and the [political ecology Meaning → Political Ecology: Study of power & politics in environmental issues.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/term/political-ecology/)  of environmental management. The **delineation** from simpler definitions involves a critical lens that questions the inherent assumptions, biases, and distributional consequences of different governance approaches.

Academically, Environmental Governance Structures are critically examined as dynamic systems shaped by power, equity, and political ecology, influencing actor interactions with nature.

The **meaning** of Environmental Governance Structures, in academic discourse, is deeply intertwined with broader debates in political science, sociology, and environmental studies. It is not merely about how to manage the environment efficiently, but also about **who** gets to decide, **whose** interests are prioritized, and **what** constitutes a just and sustainable environmental future. The **import** here is to move beyond technocratic solutions and engage with the political, social, and ethical dimensions of environmental governance.

For **elucidation**, consider the concept of environmental justice, which highlights the disproportionate burden of environmental risks faced by marginalized communities. Academic analysis of Environmental Governance Structures often critically examines how these structures may perpetuate or exacerbate existing inequalities.

![Earthy mortar structures, arranged circularly on moss, embody a sustainable art installation. The use of natural materials and repurposed objects emphasizes environmental awareness, promoting waste reduction and a circular economy. This design reduces environmental impact, fostering ecological balance and showcasing sustainability in art through biodegradable resources.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-art-installation-circular-economy-mortars-showcase-environmental.jpg "Sustainable Art Installation Circular Economy Mortars Showcase Environmental Awareness and Waste Reduction Impact")

### In-Depth Academic Perspectives

To achieve an academic level of **explication**, we must delve into specific analytical frameworks and research areas:

At the academic level, the **meaning** of Environmental Governance Structures transcends operational effectiveness to encompass critical reflection on power, justice, ethics, and cultural context. The **sense** is that a truly robust understanding requires engaging with diverse theoretical perspectives, rigorous empirical analysis, and a commitment to normative inquiry. The **substance** of academic engagement with Environmental Governance Structures lies in pushing the boundaries of knowledge, challenging conventional wisdom, and contributing to more just and sustainable environmental futures. The **connotation**, therefore, shifts towards critical inquiry, intellectual rigor, and the pursuit of deeper understanding of the complex interplay between society and environment.

Academic Environmental Governance Structures analysis is characterized by critical inquiry, ethical reflection, and rigorous empirical assessment within diverse cultural and political contexts.

--- SOURCE 4: Environmental governance: A practical framework to guide design ---
URL: https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600

SUMMARY:
Governance is one of the most important factors for ensuring effective environmental management and conservation actions. Indeed, interest in environmental governance has led to research at all scales from the local to the global and focused on issues such as resource scarcity and conflicts, allocation and access, and biodiversity conservation in forest, agricultural, freshwater, marine, and even atmospheric systems. For example, environmental governance scholars have developed theory in the areas of common-pool resource governance (Agrawal, 2003; Ostrom, 1999), adaptive governance (Armitage, Berkes, & Doubleday, 2010; Brunner, 2005; Folke et al., 2005), anticipatory governance (Boyd, Nykvist, Borgström, & Stacewicz, 2015), institutional governance (Adger, Brown, & Tompkins, 2005; Paavola, 2007), good governance (Graham, Amos, & Plumtree, 2003; Lockwood et al., 2010), and global environmental governance (O'Neill, 2009; Young, 1997) to name but a few subfields.

FULL CONTENT:

Abstract
--------

Governance is one of the most important factors for ensuring effective environmental management and conservation actions. Yet, there is still a relative paucity of comprehensive and practicable guidance that can be used to frame the evaluation, design, and analysis of systems of environmental governance. This conceptual review and synthesis article seeks to addresses this problem through resituating the broad body of governance literature into a practical framework for environmental governance. Our framework builds on a rich history of governance scholarship to propose that environmental governance has four general aims or objectives – to be effective, to be equitable, to be responsive, and to be robust. Each of these four objectives need to be considered simultaneously across the institutional, structural, and procedural elements of environmental governance. Through a review of the literature, we developed a set of attributes for each of these objectives and relate these to the overall capacity, functioning, and performance of environmental governance. Our aim is to provide a practical and adaptable framework that can be applied to the design, evaluation, and analysis of environmental governance in different social and political contexts, to diverse environmental problems and modes of governance, and at a range of scales.

1 INTRODUCTION
--------------

While environmental problems are often viewed as having technical, managerial, or behavioral dimensions, increasing attention has been paid to environmental governance as an overarching means to address these complexities. Indeed, interest in environmental governance has led to research at all scales from the local to the global and focused on issues such as resource scarcity and conflicts, allocation and access, and biodiversity conservation in forest, agricultural, freshwater, marine, and even atmospheric systems. One broad and enduring insight from this research is that governance is one of the most important factors in enabling or undermining the effectiveness of conservation and environmental management (Armitage, de Loë, & Plummer, [2012](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0008); Lockwood, Davidson, Curtis, Stratford, & Griffith, [2010](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0046); Ostrom, [1999](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0056)). Yet, we argue that there is still a relative paucity of comprehensive and practicable guidance that can be used to frame the evaluation, design, and analysis of systems of environmental governance.

This is a bold claim to make regarding a field that is as broad as it is deep. This is especially so as the academic literature on environmental governance has produced a plethora of governance theories and analytical frameworks. For example, environmental governance scholars have developed theory in the areas of common-pool resource governance (Agrawal, [2003](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0004); Ostrom, [1999](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0056)), adaptive governance (Armitage, Berkes, & Doubleday, [2010](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0007); Brunner, [2005](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0024); Folke et al., [2005](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0036)), anticipatory governance (Boyd, Nykvist, Borgström, & Stacewicz, [2015](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0023)), institutional governance (Adger, Brown, & Tompkins, [2005](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0003); Paavola, [2007](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0058)), good governance (Graham, Amos, & Plumtree, [2003](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0038); Lockwood et al., [2010](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0046)), and global environmental governance (O'Neill, [2009](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0054); Young, [1997](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0072)) to name but a few subfields. A prevailing sentiment across these literatures is that of “good” governance – or that the evaluation of environmental governance is inherently normative. Our particular aim then is to garner from these diverse areas of theory to characterize key features of governance (i.e., objectives and attributes) that can be applied to the design, evaluation, and analysis of environmental governance. We do so while accepting that it is beyond the scope of this article to provide a detailed review of this extensive theoretical literature.

Several notable challenges to the uptake and application of insights from governance scholarship are evident and need to be addressed if this body of work is to improve conservation and environmental management. First, the field as a whole can be quite theoretical, and thus seem overwhelming and inaccessible to many policymakers, managers, practitioners, and scientists from other fields who might wish to apply governance concepts, theories, or frameworks to help ameliorate real-world environmental problems. Second, there is often a lack of conceptual and analytical clarity about the difference between governance and management in much of the recent applied research on the topic (Lockwood, [2010](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0045)). Third, many of the past studies that focus on evaluating or analyzing environmental governance often focus on a limited set of features rather than considering the wider array of governance objectives and related attributes (Table[1](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-tbl-0001 "Link to table")). This may be due to the adherence by different researchers to different governance theories (e.g., adaptive governance, good governance) or frameworks (e.g., the social-ecological systems framework) and the application of the specific factors or particular indicators that they propose. While there is significant overlap, lack of integration across governance theories has meant that a more comprehensive analytical framework is still needed. Finally, past research has often focused on normative or procedural considerations (e.g., participation, recognition, access to justice) rather than substantive concerns (e.g., ecological and social outcomes) related to different governance regimes. This has meant that the links between governance capacity, functioning, and performance are often not clear – though some recent empirical research has emerged to examine and clarify the links between governance inputs and processes and social and ecological outcomes (Bodin, [2017](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0018); Cohen, Evans, & Mills, [2012](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0030); Plummer, Baird et al., [2017](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0060)).

Table 1. Objectives, attributes, characteristics, outputs and outcomes of environmental governance

| Objectives | Attributes (Qualities or Capacities) | General Characteristics or Inputs (Capacity) | Idealized Outputs (Functioning) | Idealized Outcomes (Performance) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Effective** Supports maintenance of system integrity and functioning. | Direction | Scope, goals and aims are comprehensive, clearly articulated and communicated to stakeholders. Clear boundaries on action and scope exist. | Defines what effective action encompasses and sets milestones for achieving success. | * Improvement in ecosystem functioning. * Greater biodiversity or species. * Increases in productivity of system or provisioning of ecosystem services. * Better environmental health. |
|  | Coordination | The roles, functions, and mandates of different governments, agencies and organizations are coordinated. A coordinating body or unit is present. | Produces system of rules for use, mechanisms for exclusion, management actions and spatial coverage that are complementary and adequate to achieve objectives. Provides a forum for discussion, debate, negotiating and resolving trade-offs. |  |
|  | Capacity | Capacity, skills and resources are sufficient and are being actively developed. Capable and visionary leadership is present. Mechanisms are present to resolve conflicts between groups. | Enables successful decision-making and the initiation, organization, implementation and evaluation of actions. |  |
|  | Informed | Planning and management decisions and actions are informed by best available information and integration of a diversity of knowledge types and systems. | Increases the likelihood that management actions will lead to effective outcomes. |  |
|  | Accountable | Procedures are present to hold governors accountable for performance of system. Mechanisms are in place to ensure that means and rationales for making decisions are transparent. | Ensures that governors act on mandated decisions and that effective actions are being taken. |  |
|  | Efficient | Efficacy guides decisions regarding management actions and deployment of resources. Time requirements of actors are reasonable. Economic costs and actions taken are commensurate with productivity of system. | Maximizes the productivity of management actions while minimizing the wasteful use of available resources. |  |
| **Equitable** Employs inclusive processes and produces fair outcomes. | Recognition | Policies and processes ensure acknowledgement of, respect for and incorporation of diverse perspectives, values, cultures and rights. Views of marginalized and vulnerable groups are considered. | Facilitates socially acceptable governance and perceptions of legitimacy. Aids in the design of management actions that are appropriate to the social context. | * Inclusion in decision-making processes. * Improved socio-economic outcomes. * Increases in quality of life or wellbeing. * More fair distribution of wealth. * Better access to justice and protection of rights. |
|  | Participation | Spaces and processes to enable participation and collective choice are present. Structures that ensure the representation and engagement of different stakeholder groups are in place. | Contributes to just power relations and decision-making processes. Leads to plans and actions that represent the interests of different groups. Allows parties to democratically debate decisions and maintain dignity. |  |
|  | Fair | Mechanisms are in place to ensure socio-economic costs and benefits are just and fairly distributed. Rights and responsibilities are shared and assigned fairly. Unequal circumstances are considered. | Ensures a fair balance of costs and benefits accrue to different groups. |  |
|  | Just | Laws and policies are present to protect local rights and mechanisms ensure that groups have access to justice. | Ensures rights (e.g., title, historical tenure, access, use, management) are not undermined and that reparations or compensation are made for past damages. |  |
| **Responsive** Enables adaptation to diverse contexts and changing conditions. | Learning | Monitoring, evaluation, reflections and communication of performance is institutionalized. Processes and platforms are in place to co-produce knowledge and enhance social and institutional memory. | Ensures that information is produced, documented, shared and informs decision-making. | * Enables the resilience of resource. * Enables the resilience of local communities. * More adaptable institutions to changing conditions. * More flexible institutions that can be altered to work in different contexts. |
|  | Anticipatory | Long-term planning and foresight thinking are institutionalized. Known and unknown risks and opportunities are considered, analyzed and planned for. | Produces plans and steps to prepare and prevent consequences of unexpected risks. Enhances knowledge, capacity and flexibility for disturbance. |  |
|  | Adaptive | Spaces for reflection and deliberation are institutionalized. Processes exist to revisit and evolve policies, institutions and adapt actions. | Ensures that management plans and actions are being actively adapted to reflect changing social-ecological contexts and new knowledge. |  |
|  | Innovative | Innovation and experimentation is encouraged and success and failures are monitored. A higher risk tolerance is embodied. | Allows change to be seen as an opportunity. Enables new and more effective ideas and actions to emerge. |  |
|  | Flexible | Policies exist that recognize the need to downscale environmental management and conservation models to fit local realities. Efforts are taken to understand and document about the diverse contexts where policies are applied and to deliberate on necessary adjustments. | Enables governance systems and management models to be adjusted to better fit with local social, cultural, political, economic and environmental contexts. |  |
| **Robust** Ensures functioning institutions persist, maintain performance and cope with perturbations and crises. | Legitimate | A collective vision shapes policies and guides actions at all scales. Institutional legitimacy is conferred (e.g., in policy) and perceived (e.g., by constituents). Governors act with integrity and consistency. Institutions are transparent. | Ascertains that there is support from above and that there is a supportive constituency. | * Institutions are strengthened and well supported. * Institutional performance and functioning is more or less consistent. * Institutions persist over time. |
|  | Connected | Networks of organizations and actors are strongly linked vertically and horizontally. Bridging organizations are present. Processes are in place to support network development, to develop social relations and to support mutual learning. | Helps to bridge between and across scales. Creates supportive community, produces social capital, fosters respect and trust and builds social memory. Encourages communication, information exchange, enables diffusion of innovations, and facilitates collaboration. |  |
|  | Nested | Tasks are assigned to appropriate levels. Decision-making authority and responsibility are conferred to the lowest level possible. Self-organization is encouraged and supported. Authority and responsibility is supported by adequate state or other outside support (legal recognition, political will, time commitment) and oversight. | Empowers appropriate entity to take necessary action. Allows also for shaping and adapting institutions and decision-making processes to different local sub-contexts (social circumstances, governance, ecologies) within larger system. |  |
|  | Polycentric | Decision-making and action taking centers in multiple places, across jurisdictions and at multiple scales interact and cohere towards a common goal. Institutions are present that are diverse and redundant - that serve similar purposes and have overlapping jurisdictions and functions. | Helps to buffer against change in one location. Ensures that the governance system does not collapse when faced with adversity or crises. |  |

This conceptual review and synthesis article seeks to address these problems through resituating the broad body of governance literature – including the languages, terms, methods, and metrics – to provide a much needed comprehensive and practical framework and a common lexicon for future engagements. Our aim is to provide a framework that can be adapted and applied to the design, evaluation, and analysis of the capacity, functioning, and performance of environmental governance in diverse contexts and at a range of scales.

2 TOWARD A COMPREHENSIVE AND PRACTICAL FRAMEWORK FOR ENVIRONMENTAL GOVERNANCE
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.1 Methods

Our first step was to reduce the complexity of the main analytical elements, objectives, and related attributes that pertain to environmental governance while still being comprehensive (Figure[1](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-fig-0001)). When developing the framework, we first reviewed the literature to ascertain clear definitions and conceptualizations of the analytical elements (i.e., institutions, structures, and processes) of governance (see below). We then reviewed the academic literature on environmental governance to develop a comprehensive list of considerations (alternately termed principles, attributes, or indicators of governance by different authors) associated with the capacity, functioning, and performance of governance. As our aim was to be comprehensive, we reviewed the literature until thematic saturation was achieved – that is, no new themes were emerging. To develop a summary list of attributes and objectives from this long list, we combined commensurate terms into a set of 19 attributes, which we checked against the literature to ensure comprehensive thematic coverage. Finally, we assigned these attributes to four overarching categories that encompass the general aims or objectives of environmental governance. In so doing, we sought to evaluate and construct each category according to guidance on designing clear and appropriate attributes and objectives. That is, we ensured they were: distinct, comprehensive, direct, operational, understandable and unambiguous (Keeney, [2007](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0041); Keeney & Gregory, [2005](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0042)). A summary of this review of the literature is in Table[1](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-tbl-0001 "Link to table") with supporting references provided throughout the text, whereas a more succinct representation of the primary objectives and attributes as they relate to the elements of governance is in the framework in Figure[1](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-fig-0001).

[![Image 7: Details are in the caption following the image](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/4131f472-9377-4cc2-bc8a-cc49bc37e74f/conl12600-fig-0001-m.png)](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/0cf92add-6448-442f-a753-85430650fef5/conl12600-fig-0001-m.jpg)

**Figure 1**

[Open in figure viewer](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#)[PowerPoint](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/action/downloadFigures?id=conl12600-fig-0001&partId=&doi=10.1111%2Fconl.12600)

A practical framework for understanding the objectives, attributes, and elements of environmental governance

### 2.2 Definition and conceptual elements of environmental governance

Governance is generally defined as the _institutions, structures, and processes_ that determine who makes decisions, how and for whom decisions are made, whether, how and what actions are taken and by whom and to what effect (Graham et al., [2003](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0038); Lockwood et al., [2010](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0046)). An important conceptual distinction needs to be made between governance and management: the latter refers to the _resources, plans, and actions_ that result from the functioning of governance (Lockwood, [2010](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0045)). The aim of environmental governance, in particular, is to manage individual behaviors or collective actions in pursuance of public environmental goods and related societal outcomes (Armitage et al., [2012](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0008); Termeer, Dewulf, & Van Lieshout, [2010](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0066)). To comprehend environmental governance is to understand how decisions related to the environment are made and whether resultant policies and processes lead to environmentally and socially sustainable outcomes. The analysis of environmental governance focuses on the capacity, functioning, and/or performance of the institutional, structural, and procedural elements of governance (Figure[1](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-fig-0001))

Drawing first on early work by North ([1990](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12600#conl12600-bib-0052)), we furt

enriched_points.expanded_content: Environmental governance structures encompass institutions, processes, and regulations guiding interactions between stakeholders and nature, ensuring effective management of natural resources and environmental protection.

Engaging diverse stakeholders, including communities, businesses, and governments, fosters collaboration, enhances transparency, and promotes shared decision-making in environmental governance, leading to more sustainable outcomes.

Incorporating scientific research into environmental policy frameworks enhances evidence-based decision-making, ensures informed actions, and helps stakeholders understand ecological impacts, fostering effective management strategies.

Robust decision-making processes involve evaluating environmental data, considering stakeholder input, and balancing economic, social, and ecological factors to achieve sustainable governance and policy effectiveness.

Sustainability initiatives aim to balance environmental protection, economic growth, and social equity, promoting long-term ecological health through practices like resource conservation, waste reduction, and renewable energy adoption.
